sentences.text: [LAUGHS] We arrived there I think some time towards the end of April, having left Germany earlier in April.
And--
Absolutely yes.
And of course, because it was run by B'nai B'rith, there was a strong Jewish atmosphere there.
We-- the food was kosher.
And we were about 60 or so boys in the hostel.
They ranged in age from 8 and 1/2 or so-- my brother was one of the four youngest-- to 16.
We had a couple of twins there.
We had a set of twins there who come from Kassel in Germany.
And they had in fact already been in a concentration camp.
They were the oldest.
I don't know on what basis they were released, but they were released.
They obviously couldn't have been soldiers in the First World War, that's for sure.
And we went to synagogue every Friday and every Sabbath morning.
And the rabbi, a very charming man with very pleasant children, told me that I would be bar mitzvah in two weeks' time.
Yes.
And that was shortly after my arrival.
And that fitted in, of course, with my birthday, because my birthday was 7th of May.
It was perfectly all right.
And I notified my aunt and uncle.
And they would, of course, be coming down to Margate to be present, to represent the family.
There was nobody else there, obviously.
And the night before, that means the Friday afternoon as it were, before my bar mitzvah, the rabbi said, he's sorry.
He'd made a mistake, but it'll be next week.
And so I had to notify my aunt and uncle very quickly by telephone.
And they said they were very, very sorry, but they'd booked many, many moons before a trip to New York on the And they didn't really feel they want to cancel it.
And naturally, I accepted that.
I was a little boy.
I had to accept whatever my seniors said to me.
And so I was there without any family at all for my bar mitzvah, which was perhaps sad, but you know, I didn't really know what bar mitzvah was all about.
Obviously, I knew the raison d'etre of it.
But whether this was to be a large family do or not or what, it really didn't concern me all that much.
I was totally ignorant of the--
No, no, no.
No.
I merely said the brachot when I was called up.
I did no reciting of a parshah or part of the sidra or the haftarah.
No, no.
I just said the brachot.
And I was called up and I was congratulated by everybody.
And in the hostel, we had an extra piece of cake that morning.
We were kept-- don't forget it was during the war.
No, no.
I beg your pardon.
It was before the war, but the hostel, the organization ran itself on a shoestring.
And rightly so, they lavished very little luxuries on us, far from it, saving any money that they had to try and get more children out of Germany rather than let us live in some sort of luxury.
And I think regretfully as it was at the time-- you know, we were all used to a certain amount of luxury-- this obviously didn't play a major part in our existence.
We [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were dressed in uniforms, as it happened to be the sort of custom in England.
We were given these special peak caps.
One of the calamities that occurred was that we were all equipped with long trousers.
Now, in Germany in those days, one didn't go into long trousers as a boy until the age of 18 or 19.
Here we were-- my brother was 8 and I was 12 or 13 by that time-- in long trousers.
First of all, the material was such that it was terribly rough on our legs, the dainty little legs that the spoiled brats had.
I always used to wear pajama trousers underneath the-- the long trousers, because my legs were totally stiff.
I couldn't sit down.
I couldn't bend my leg, I felt terrible.
We were allowed-- and we had a blazer.
And there was a badge on here that indicated Rowden Hall School, which was the name of the thing.
It was all quite pleasant and so on.
And we were allowed to go-- Margate was, of course, a seaside resort not too far away from London.
And at weekends, people came from London and relaxed for a couple of days or perhaps for a day.
In those days, working on Saturdays was still very, very common.
It was the order of the-- the day, as it were.
We were allowed to walk around on the beach.
The house was right by the seafront, in a very beautiful position.
And we were allowed to go into town and do whatever we wanted to do.
But on Saturdays and Sundays, we were, as it were, confined to barracks.
And on inquiries why that was so, we were being told, which was painful, that a lot of very hard-working people came down from London to relax.
And they didn't want to be reminded of the terrible things that were happening over on the continent.
And that quite honestly, even at the age of 13, was very painful, very painful, very, very painful.

Here we were, and suddenly those people who'd give us a haven, who'd saved our lives literally, didn't nevertheless want to know about the terrible things that were going on to not only our parents, our relatives, the Jewish people in general.
And Margate being more or less at the extension of the English Channel became, of course, very much a focal point during the evacuation of Dunkirk.
And Hitl-- Chamberlain declared war at nine-- sorry, at 11 o'clock on that fateful Sunday morning, the 3rd of September, 1939.
By the grace of God, my parents reached the shores, reached Britain 36 hours before Chamberlain declared war.
Germany had already broken into Poland.
The war was already raging in Poland.
And they managed to get us into England 36 hours before the war started, an absolute miracle.
And so to finish that particular aspect of it, they both died a natural death here in England.
Yes, indeed.
But at least they were out of Germany and they were safe and they were free.
Well, as big as one can have it, yes.
Certainly we had a very big reunion.
My aunt and uncle were there.
And of course, my parents were there, naturally, and my brother and myself.
And that was our family.
And of course, we feared for all the other many, many members who were left behind in Germany, yes.
Yes.
No, we didn't.
They-- they came over here and were very much restricted as to how much money they were allowed to bring out of Germany.
It was a pittance.
And so again my aunt and uncle took care of them financially.
And because we lived in a hostel in Margate, they organized for a very small flat for my parents in Margate so that at least we could see each other as often as possible.
My mother then came to the hostel every day and helped with the repairing of socks and doing laundry and all that sort of thing for the others.
And my father very often took on the place of deputy headmaster.
When the headmaster had a day off or so, my father would come and would take care of-- in a nominal sort of fashion of the administration and so on and so forth.
They tried to help as much as they could naturally.
And we were very, very fortunate, my brother and I.
We were the only, the only children amongst those 16 who had this-- this terrifically fortunate situation at hand.

The vast majority of the parents of all those with whom I was at Rowden Hall ultimately died in concentration camps, as did, of course, the largest number of my own relatives, uncles, aunts, grandfather, and so on and so forth.
Grandfathers, I had two grandfathers.
A very good friend of mine had his parents on a boat that left Hamburg I think at the last day of August and was destined from Hamburg to Southampton.
It was full of refugees, all leaving Germany legally, and entering Britain quite legally.
All the papers and all the tickets and everything else was in perfect order from both countries' points of view.

But unfortunately, that ship, which was a German ship, was in fact on the high seas when-- on the way to Southampton when Hitler-- sorry, when Chamberlain declared war on Germany on that fateful Sunday morning.
And naturally, it being a German ship, it went back to Hamburg.
It couldn't go to Southampton.
And I understand that all these people who were on board, some 800 of-- or so of them, were all eliminated within a very, very short time.
No.
We didn't know that at the time.
My friend didn't know it at the time.
I think he only found that out at the end of the war, when to some extent some of the pictures or some of the events became known.
Oh, yes.
Now, the war having started in '39, the Allies, as you know, didn't do too well.
The Germans were incredibly successful, occupied France, occupied Belgium and occupied Holland.
And the British Expeditionary Force obviously had to fall back and ultimately ended up in this very glorious retreat of Dunkirk.
And because Margate was on the front line or very close to the Channel, Margate was one of the reception ports, if I can call it that, of troops coming over and disembarking in Margate.
And they were received and taken care of by nurses and other volunteers, mainly female, many men as well.
And schoolchildren were asked to help as well.
And naturally, this seemed to be a wonderful chance for me.
And so I was seconded to a nurse.
And oddly enough, I was equipped with thousands of cigarettes.
In those days, everybody smoked Woodbines.
They came in small green packets.
And my task was to light a cigarette and then put it into the mouth of the oncoming soldier.
Everybody was smoking in those days.

And this was a very grim experience, a beaten army, because that's what they were, terribly bedraggled, many of them with head wounds and bandages and so on and so forth, many who had to be helped ashore by colleagues, many of them with hardly any uniform left.
There were quite a lot of-- of top half at least naked soldiers coming on.
And they were so demoralized that I remember they took their steel helmets and their First World War rifles, Lee-Enfields, and threw those into the sea as a sign of disgust and total abjection.
And I was lighting cigarettes.

Because at the age of 13 I was not an experienced smoker and cigarettes were not as sophisticated as they are today with tips, the-- my-- the skin on my lips were very, very raw, because with every cigarette there went a little bit of my skin on the cigarette into the soldier's mouth.
And this went on for about three days.
And I must have got through, oh, easily one-and-a-half-thousand cigarettes of so.
[LAUGHS] And my lips were incredibly raw.
But you know, this was part of the war effort.
We all had to make a sacrifice.
I wasn't particularly worried about it.
And in the end, you know, it all righted itself, obviously.
My introduction to cigarettes, we were then very shortly afterwards evacuated, because by that time, the hostel was in a sad state.
We had no windows left.
They were all blown out by bomb blasts.
We were never hit directly by a bomb, but the Germans naturally were trying to hamper the retreat as much as they possibly could.
And so they bombed harbor installations and so on and so forth.
So-- and that state of blown-out windows and not enough money or materials to repair this sort of thing, that was something that had been going on for a few months, as a matter of fact.
And since it was, you know, early spring or so I suppose when it started, I remember having some snow in the bedroom, having snowball battles [LAUGHS] from bed to bed.
Yeah.
We were evacuated from Margate.
And we were sent to central England, in the Midlands, to an area which was fundamentally a coal mining area in South Staffordshire.
And there, we were picked up by a family, a coal miner and his wife, who predominantly wanted girls.
Everybody wanted girls in the house.
They thought, I suppose rightly, that they were more tidy, more orderly.
They were possibly more useful with regards to helping domestically and so on.
But since we were about 50 or 60 boys was-- from the hostel, there was a predominance of boys there.
And in the end, we found we were sort of selected like you go through an auction and say I'll have this chair.
I'll have that sort of bed.
And so we were picked.
I insisted that we have to stay together, my brother and I.
We came into a family which was not very pleasant.
There was some matrimonial trouble between the husband and the wife.
We saw occasionally the knives and plates being thrown at each other.
They had a little boy, who they thought highly of.
It was to that child quite understandable.
But very often, he devoured much of our rations.
We invariably had dry bread while he had some margarine on his-- on his-- on his bread.
And any attempt to try and get into a better foster home, as it were, was totally, totally unsuccessful.
The billeting officer was a-- a-- a teacher, an old teacher at the school to which we were attached.
Well, yes.
We could make ourselves understood and we could understand things.
You know, at that age, it's basically not very difficult for children to pick up a foreign language.
I was unfortunate that going back to my grammar school education in Breslau, the only foreign language I was taught were French and Hebrew.
And so English was a totally new language to us.
And I suppose that applied to many other children as well.
Yes.
We could make ourselves understood.
We could even read the newspaper and so on and so forth.
And when I became in the top class, which was a class of 14-- 13- and 14-year-olds-- the school was of a very low educational level-- I was asked to become prefect.
And shortly after that I became head prefect.
And once I was head prefect and I confronted the billeting officer once again--
Who was a teacher, I was a demigod to him.
He said, oh, yes.
We have lots of wonderful places here, there, and everywhere.
And he gave me the choice of two or three.
And the one that we went to look at first-- and when I say we, it was this very good friend of mine, Herman, and myself.
Yes.
Herman.
Yes.
From the hostel, and who was evacuated together with me.
Also a refugee boy.
Yes.
Yes indeed.
Yes.
And it was he who lost his parents on that tragic boat.
Yes.
Hirschberger.
Yes.
And he and I on our bicycles-- we'd acquired some means of locomotion by that time-- went to have a look at this particular billet, again a coal-mining family.
But they lived in a cottage a little aside from the village as such.
And as we rode into the-- into the main drive, we heard some pigs grunting away in a pigsty.
And somehow, that doesn't put us off.
We had a fantastic welcome.
To cut a very long story short, we were made-- both my brother Walter and I were made most welcome.
We became members of the family.
And we were and still are in touch, of course, with that family.
Stotten.
Mr. and Mrs.
Stotten.
And they're both dead now, but they were wonderful people and so were their family and so were all their friends, who fundamentally were all coal miners.
Well, it was all new to them.
And to them, as to all those people in the early stages when we arrived there in the summer of 1940, we were Germans.
And they didn't understand the difference between Germans and Jews.
They may have heard something about atrocities against Jews.
But to them, we were all Germans.
And it was a bit difficult for them to be fighting a war against Germany and suddenly taking care of German children.

The questions that I was being asked quite often was, how many times did you see Hitler, you know, and so on and so forth, quite naive and absurd questions, but perhaps understandable under the circumstances.

And it took us a long time to try and persuade them that, yes, unfortunately, we were born in Germany, but nevertheless we were Jewish and we were victims of their persecution and we were just as much interested in winning this war as they were, because it was our war just as much it was theirs.

The only difference was that they were grown-up men and they were digging for victory in the coal mines, and we were still kids and couldn't, in fact, serve in any specific way, either in the forces or even in the coal mines.
Oh, very much so, yes.
By that time, the villagers had begun to realize, you know, about six months after we'd arrive there originally, what in fact our status really was.
Oh, yes, of course.
Because the area was on the outer perimeter of the Birmingham, which as you know was a major manufacturing center, the major city, there were a number of anti-aircraft batteries around there.
And during air raids on Birmingham, then quite clearly we were forced to go into shelters in the same way as everybody else.
Yes, of course we witnessed the war, as civilians did and in those sort of areas.
We were, of course, very keenly interested in the progress of how the war was going.
And in those early days, the war was not going too well, as far as we were concerned.
Germany was invading more and more countries.
And the situation was looking grimmer and grimmer all the time.
I did, yes, because my parents were in England.
And my father was interned on the Isle of Man as an enemy alien.
He was, after all, of German nationality.
Though he was Jewish and was-- in fact had been in a German concentration camp, the law was that aliens, any male aliens by definition had-- had to be in fact locked up.
And that was understandable.
They were in camps.
They were not mistreated.
But they were away from their activities, from their wives and so on and so forth.
My mother also, because her nationality was German, had to leave Margate, which was considered to be a front line city.
If there would have been an invasion, which was very much on the books in those days, they obviously would have landed in the south of England.
And so all aliens had to be cleared out of this area.
My mother had to live in London.
And there she joined the ARP, which was an air raid-- you know, the Air Raid Precautions organization.
And she had a very fascinating experience on one occasion when a German pilot was shot down, came down by parachute and get himself entangled in the guttering of a house.
And the chief air raid warden was very concerned about the safety and told my mother, would you please tell him not to worry.
We're getting a ladder up and we're bringing a cup of tea up to him, you see.
My mother said, you must be out of your mind.
That is a German swine up there.
[LAUGHS] Well, anyway, a long [LAUGHS] argument.
And he gave my mother an order.
Mrs.
Mendelsson, he said, this is an order.
And having been brought up in Germany, an order was an order.
And so she reluctantly obeyed the order and told this Nazi, don't worry.
We're bringing up a cup of tea and we'll get you to safety.
Is it?
So soon already.
[LAUGHS]
